summary: Gruber explains why third-party USB-C to Lightning cables don't yet exist — Apple hasn't released the new C94 Lightning connectors supporting USB Power Delivery to MFI licensees, with availability expected mid-2019. He then makes a detailed case that the iPhone will never switch from Lightning to USB-C, arguing Apple wants to maintain MFI control over peripherals, most users prefer keeping their existing cables, and Lightning is physically smaller and more elegant. He predicts iPhones will keep Lightning until wireless charging is good enough to eliminate ports entirely, and that non-Pro iPads will also stay on Lightning. He also examines Apple's ecosystem of Lightning-powered gadgets, particularly AirPods, as a signal of Apple's connector intentions.
key_points: Third-party USB-C to Lightning cables require a new C94 connector from Apple that supports USB Power Delivery at 18W, and these won't be available to MFI licensees until mid-2019
Apple's MFI program gives it effective veto power over iPhone peripherals like battery cases with headphone jacks — control it would lose with USB-C
The mass market doesn't want connector changes; the backlash over the 30-pin to Lightning switch in 2012 showed most users see new ports as a money grab, not progress
Lightning is physically smaller and more elegantly designed than USB-C, and iPhones don't need USB-C's additional capabilities
Gruber predicts iPhones will keep Lightning until Apple removes ports entirely in favor of wireless charging
money_quotes: I don't think that's going to happen, ever.

The nerd world may clamor for one universal connector that charges everything from iPhones to iPads to MacBooks, but the normal world just wants their existing cables to keep working when they buy a new iPhone.

For however many iPhone users there are who are upset that iPhones continue to use the proprietary Lightning port when they could, technically, use USB-C instead, I would bet big money there are way more who just want Apple to keep using Lightning because they already have Lightning cables everywhere they need them.
I think iPhones will stick with Lightning until wireless charging is fast enough that Apple can remove all ports, Apple Watch-style.
Lightning is a simple, elegant male/female design. USB-C, like all previous USB versions, is a weird male connector with female slot / female port with a tiny little male connector inside.
Personally, I would never trust these uncertified cables.
If the iPhone were to switch to USB-C, I don't think they could stop anyone from making USB-C battery cases. I do not think Apple will cede this control.
Spec-knowledgeable nerds have trouble believing this, but many iPhone users love the wimpy 5W charger Apple includes with iPhones because it's so small.
key_insight: Apple will keep Lightning on iPhones not because USB-C lacks technical merit, but because Lightning gives Apple peripheral control, avoids alienating mainstream users, and will eventually be bypassed entirely by wireless charging.
